input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

data.content: Both runtimes are available. I'll create a BenchmarkDotNet project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0.
data.reasoningText:
Both .NET 9 and .NET 10 runtimes are available. I'll create a BenchmarkDotNet project that multi-targets net9.0 and net10.0 to benchmark the three methods. This is the cleanest way to compare performance across frameworks.

Let me create the benchmark project.

+  <PropertyGroup>
+    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>

+  </ItemGroup>
+  <ItemGroup>

+  </ItemGroup>
+</Project>
+

data.content:
Benchmarks completed. Here are the results comparing .NET 9 vs .NET 10:

```
| Method              | Runtime  | Mean      | Allocated |
|---------------------|----------|----------:|----------:|
| AverageWordLength   | .NET 9   |  4.756 μs |       0 B |
| AverageWordLength   | .NET 10  |  1.269 μs |       0 B |
| WordLengthHistogram | .NET 9   |  1.900 μs |      88 B |
| WordLengthHistogram | .NET 10  |  3.001 μs |      88 B |
| UniqueWordCount     | .NET 9   |  3.723 μs |   3384 B |
| UniqueWordCount     | .NET 10  | 14.226 μs |   3384 B |
```

**⚠️ Important caveat:** The standard deviations are very high relative to the means (30-50%), which strongly suggests this machine is under heavy load from other processes. The noisy environment makes these results unreliable — the apparent differences between .NET 9 and .NET 10 are likely within the margin of error. Memory allocations (which are unaffected by load) are identical across both runtimes, which is expected since the code is the same.

I'd recommend re-running these benchmarks on a quieter machine to get trustworthy timing numbers. The benchmark project is at `TextAnalyzer.Benchmarks/` and can be re-run with:

```
dotnet run -c Release --framework net10.0 -- --filter '*'
```
